---
layout: default
title: "From SQLite to R"
date: 2026-01-01 ## Change the date to release data

notebook: running
chapter: 4

project: Running

summary: >
  Abstract

status: published ## Turn to published when published
sitemap: false ## Turn to true when published

image: /images/notebooks/running/main.png


keywords:
  -


linkedin:
github:
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_knit$set(
  base.dir = normalizePath("../.."),
  base.url = "/"
)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  eval = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.path = "images/notebooks/running/",
  fig.width = 8,
  fig.height = 5,
  dpi = 150
)
```

<div class="investigation-progress">
    <p class="investigation-progress-title">
        Running notebook: Investigation progress
    </p>

    <ul>
        <li>Getting hold of the data</li>
        <li>Using a tool discovered on GitHub</li>
        <li>Understanding how data are structured</li>
    </ul>
</div>



This chapter marks our first contact with the date themselves. so far, we've mainly downloaded and curated them, but didn't really *look* at them.

Today we do, from our R console.

I'm about to show you how to interact with the SQLite databases we obtained from GarminDB
So see this chapter as bringing the last building block we need before starting analysing.


# Introduction

The database now makes sense, but it is far too large to analyse as it is.

and there are too many different activities stored together.

We need a way to retrieve only the observations relevant to our project.

# doing everything in R

```{r test-plot}
plot(1:10)
```

## connecting to db

Explain that the connection is just a doorway.

## looking around

```{r list-tables, eval=FALSE}
dbListTables()
```

```{r glimpse-activities, eval=FALSE}
glimpse(tbl(con, "activities"))
```

## Lazy evaluation

```{r create-lazy-activities-table, eval=FALSE}
activities <-
    tbl(con, "activities")
```

    doesn't retrieve anything

```{r collect-activities, eval=FALSE}
collect()
```

# Showcase some data extractions



# When dbplyr isn’t enough

Show how data can be extracted using SQL directly.


# Wrap-up

This is it. We now have everything we need to start investigating our data.
